coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon dot gated hollow mesoporous carbon for chemo-photothermal synergistic therapy
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xiudan
Lin, Yuanzhe
Li, Xian
Wang, Da
Di, Donghua
Zhao, Qinfu
Wang, Siling
coredata.dc:description: An efficient and intelligent nano-carrier that combines cell imaging with near infrared (NIR) light and redox dual-responsive drug delivery was successfully prepared. The hollow mesoporous carbon (HMC) nanoparticles with high photothermal conversion ability were developed to increase the drug loading efficiency and realize chemotherapy and photothermal synergetic therapy. The photo-stable and luminescent carbon dots (CDs) were prepared from branched polyethyleneimine (PEI) by hydrothermal reaction. The PEI CDs (CDPEI) were grafted on the openings of HMC as the “gatekeepers” via disulfide units (HMC-SS-CDPEI) to prevent the premature release of doxorubicin (DOX). In the presence of GSH, the CDPEI separated from HMC due to the breakage of disulfide bonds, thus triggering the rapid release of the encapsulated drug. In addition, the release rate of DOX could be further accelerated by NIR light irradiation due to the increased temperature which would decrease the interaction between HMC and DOX. The fluorescence of the CDPEI is quenched when being attached to the HMC, while it is recovered when the CDPEI breaking away from HMC. Hence, the fluorescent CDPEI not only act as a gatekeeper to control drug release but also play a vital role in monitoring the process of the drug delivery. The developed HMC-SS-CDPEI showed dual-responsive drug release property and could be used as visible nano-platforms for chemo-photothermal synergistic therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hollow mesoporous carbon
Carbon dots
Thermal-therapy
Bioimaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Study of cellular photothermal effect induced by NIR
MTT activity assay for measuring NIR-induced cytotoxicity
Assessment of cellular uptake by confocal laser scanning microscopy
The drug existing state and loading yield of HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX
Evaluation of in vitro photothermal effect studies induced by NIR
The advantage of HMC-SS-CDPEI after grafting of CDPEI

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Doctoral Research Funding of Liaoning Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81603058 and 81473165 ), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2017M611270 ), Career Development Program for Young Teachers in Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (No. ZQN2016025 ) and Doctoral Research Funding of Liaoning Province (No. 201601146 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of CDPEI; (B) enlarged image of CDPEI; (C) the corresponding Fast Fourier Transformation (FFT) of the CDPEI; (D) XPS spectra of CDPEI. And the AFM images of CDPEI. (E) AFM 3D image; (F) AFM topography image and (G) height profile along the line in (F).

(A) The UV–vis absorption spectra, excitation and emission spectra of CDPEI (inset: the photos of the aqueous solution of CDPEI in the bright field and UV light at 360nm), and (B) PL emission spectra of CDPEI under excitation with light of different wavelengths.

(A) The nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms and (B) pore size distribution curves of HMC, HMC-COOH and HMC-SS-CDPEI; (C) the corresponding zeta potentials of HMC-COOH, HMC-SS-Py, HMC-SS-COOH and HMC-SS-CDPEI; (D) the FT-IR spectra of HMC, HMC-COOH, HMC-SS-COOH and HMC-SS-CDPEI.

(A) NIR-triggered heat generation of HMC-SS-CDPEI suspensions at different concentrations irradiated at the power density of 2W and (B) NIR-triggered heat generation of 100μg/mL HMC-SS-CDPEI suspensions irradiated at different laser power densities.
Cumulative release behaviors of HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX in pH 5.0PBS and pH 7.4PBS with the addition of 10mMGSH.
Release profiles of DOX/HMC-SS-CDPEI with NIR irradiation for 3min at the power density of 4W in (A) pH 7.4PBS and (B) pH 5.0PBS.

(A) Size distribution of HMC and HMC-SS-CDPEI; (B) fluorescence spectra of CDPEI and the supernatant PL intensity of HMC-SS-CDPEI after adding 0mM, 1mM, 5mM and 10mMGSH at 350nm excitation (inset: the photo of the supernatants from HMC-SS-CDPEI under the UV light at 360nm with or without 10mMGSH); and (C) images of HMC-COOH and HMC-SS-CDPEI dispersed in water and pH 7.4PBS with the concentration of 2mg/mL.
NIR-induced cellular photothermal effect of 4T1 cells incubated with different concentrations of HMC-SS-CDPEI under the NIR irradiation for 3min at 2W.
The viability of 4T1 cells incubated with various concentrations of blank HMC-SS-CDPEI, free DOX and HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX with or without NIR laser irradiation. (*p<0.05, **p<0.01, ***p<0.001).
CLSM images of 4T1 cells cultivated with (A) DOX and (B) HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX at 37°C for 2h under different excitation wavelengths of 408 and 561nm. (bar represents 50μm).

Schematic illustration of (A) synthetic route of HMC-SS-CDPEI; (B) drug loading and the redox-responsive and NIR-induced release property, and (C) NIR light-mediated nanoplatforms of HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX for cancer thermo-chemotherapy.
The adsorption–desorption characteristic parameters of HMC, HMC-COOH, and HMC-SS-CDPEI.
IC50 value for HMC-SS-CDPEI, DOX and HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX samples for preventing 4T1 cells growth after culturing for 24h.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon dot gated hollow mesoporous carbon for chemo-photothermal synergistic therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics, School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road, Shenyang, Liaoning Province 110016, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An efficient and intelligent nano-carrier that combines cell imaging with near infrared (NIR) light and redox dual-responsive drug delivery was successfully prepared. The hollow mesoporous carbon (HMC) nanoparticles with high photothermal conversion ability were developed to increase the drug loading efficiency and realize chemotherapy and photothermal synergetic therapy. The photo-stable and luminescent carbon dots (CDs) were prepared from branched polyethyleneimine (PEI) by hydrothermal reaction. The PEI CDs (CDPEI) were grafted on the openings of HMC as the “gatekeepers” via disulfide units (HMC-SS-CDPEI) to prevent the premature release of doxorubicin (DOX). In the presence of GSH, the CDPEI separated from HMC due to the breakage of disulfide bonds, thus triggering the rapid release of the encapsulated drug. In addition, the release rate of DOX could be further accelerated by NIR light irradiation due to the increased temperature which would decrease the interaction between HMC and DOX. The fluorescence of the CDPEI is quenched when being attached to the HMC, while it is recovered when the CDPEI breaking away from HMC. Hence, the fluorescent CDPEI not only act as a gatekeeper to control drug release but also play a vital role in monitoring the process of the drug delivery. The developed HMC-SS-CDPEI showed dual-responsive drug release property and could be used as visible nano-platforms for chemo-photothermal synergistic therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: There has been a rapid development of anticancer nanomedicine in recent years. However, limitations are becoming more and more apparent, such as severe toxicity, side effects, poor bioavailability and large dosage requirement. Thus we want to design a multifunctional drug delivery platforms (MFDDPs) to achieve controlled and targeted drug delivery that can improve treatment efficacy to a large extent.

Photothermal therapy is a physical therapy, in which light could be converted into cytotoxic heat to eliminate tumor cells [1]. Near-infrared (NIR) light, working asa burgeoning in vitro physical method shows great promise in assisting drug delivery and improving therapeutic efficacy [2]. NIR light with a wavelength from 700 to 1100nm, is noninvasive for normal tissues and possesses a long penetration depth [3]. To achieve better ablation of tumor cells and overcome side effects caused by traditional chemical drugs, the combination of chemotherapy and photothermal therapy which is called chemo-photothermal therapy has attracted wide attention [4,5]. The novel combination of therapy can induce synergistic effects by delivering cytotoxic heat and chemical agents to cancer sites synchronously and locally [6]. In addition, under the circumstance of high temperature, the toxicity of chemical drugs are improved [7] and tumor cells are more sensitive to chemotherapeutic agents [8], thus the dosage requirement and side effects are reduced to a large extent.

Up to now, a wide variety of functional materials have been explored as NIR-absorbing drug carriers. Among them carbon nanomaterials such as fullerene, carbon nanotubes [9–11] and graphene [12–14] have attracted wide attention. Mesoporous carbon nanoparticles (MCN), as an original carbon-based carrier exhibit excellent great potential working as MFDDPs due to their unique physiochemical properties, such as the high photothermal conversion capability, large surface area, tunable pore size, low toxicity, and easily-modified surface [15–17]. The attractive channels are especially important for drug loading and protecting therapeutic agents inside the mesopores. Compared with conventional MCN, hollow mesoporous carbon (HMC) with a hollow core inside the carrier show higher drug encapsulation efficiency. Moreover, HMC showed better photothermal conversion efficacy than MCN [18].

Premature drug release is another obstacle that we need to work out. Fortunately, stimuli-responsive MFDDPs have made great progress in recent years. A key advantage of such MFDDPs is their capacity to regulate drug release behavior under specific stimuli, which can largely improve the treatment efficacy at the tumor sites and reduce systemic side effects. Stimuli including pH [19], redox potential [20], light [21], temperature, and enzymes [22] could trigger drug release at the specific target tissues. Among these stimuli, redox potential [23] is popular intracellular stimulus at cancer sites. There is a significant difference in the glutathione (GSH) concentration between the extracellular (2μM) and intracellular (10mM) environments [22]. And the concentration of intracellular GSH levels in most tumors is at least 4 times higher than that in normal cells.

Recently, carbon dots (CDs) asa new class of fluorescent materials have attracted many researchers’ attention due to their superior properties such as good luminescence [24], low toxicity [25], photo-stability, hydrophilic and biocompatibility [26]. Thus, it could be explored as bio-sensing and bio-imaging [27,28] candidate to monitor the drug delivery process [29]. Moreover, controllable size made it working as gatekeepers of mesopores that can prevent drug leakage efficiently. Considering the unique properties of HMC, NIR and CDs, it is desirable integrate NIR light and CDs with the biocompatible stimuli-responsive HMC to develop novel type of MFDDPs.

In this work, HMC was used as the drug carrier as well as the NIR absorbing material to realize the synergistic treatment of chemotherapy and photothermal therapy. The fluorescent carbon dots CDPEI with a diameter of 3nm was fabricated using the hydrothermal polymerization method. The CDPEI were grafted on the mesoporous openings of HMC via disulfide bonds to prevent the premature drug release as shown in Scheme 1 . Doxorubicin (DOX) was employed as model drug to investigate drug loading capacity and stimuli-responsive drug release property of HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX. Moreover, the supernatant fluorescence intensity with or without GSH were explored to understand mechanism of redox-responsive release behavior. Stability, hemolysis and cytotoxicity assays were performed to evaluate biocompatibility of HMC-SS-CDPEI. Furthermore, cell viability experiments were taken to estimate the synergistic effects of HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX by combination of chemotherapy and phototherapy. All in all, the novel MFDDPs can not only achieve better inhibition of tumor cells, but also serve as a good platform for real-time imaging of nano-carriers.

Tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) and cetyltrimethylammoniumbromide (CTAB) were supplied by Tianjin Bodi Chemical Industry Co. The branched polyethyleneimine (PEI), 3-mercaptopropionic, glutathione (GSH), 3-mercaptoprhopyltrimethoxysilane (MPTMS, 95%), N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) and doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) were obtained from Aladdin Chemical Inc. (Shanghai, China). Cell culture medium dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM), trypsin-EDTA solution (0.25%), fetal bovine serum (FBS) and penicillin–streptomycin were obtained from GIBCO, Invitrogen Co. (Carlsbad, NM, USA). 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) were supplied by Amresco (USA). Fluorescent 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) was obtained from Biyuntian Co. (Nanjing, China). All other chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

The functionalized CDPEI are prepared by a route as reported previously [26]. 0.1g branched PEI (Mw 25kDa) was dissolved in 20mL deionized water. Then, 0.4g citric acid (CA) was added to obtain a homogeneous solution under vigorous stirring at room temperature. Subsequently, it is transferred to an autoclave and kept at 180°C for 10h. Finally, the resulting solution was centrifuged at 15000rpm to produce a bright yellow solution which is then dialyzed against deionized water for 1day.

The synthesis of S-(2-Aminoethylthio)-2-thiopyridine hydrochloride (Py-SS-NH2) was performed on the basis of our previous work [30]. And HMC-COOH was prepared by the previous method [31]. Firstly, HMC-COOH (100mg) was dispersed in 30mL PBS (pH = 7.4) and mixed with NHS and EDC for 1h under stirring at room temperature. Secondly, Py-SS-NH2 (40mg) was added to the above suspension and stirred for 12h. Afterwards, the mixture solution was centrifuged at 13000rpm for 15min and supernatant was discarded. The precipitates were washed with purified water and ethanol in sequence. The products were named as HMC-SS-Py.

The preparation of HMC-SS-COOH was performed by dispersing 80mg of HMC-SS-Py in 25mL ethanol. Subsequently, 3-mercaptopropionic acid (40μL) was added into the above suspension and the reaction mixture was stirred for 24h. Finally, HMC-SS-COOH was obtained by a centrifugation, washed with ethanol and dried under vacuum.

First, 20mg DOX was dissolved in 8mL distilled water, and 20mg HMC-SS-COOH was added to the DOX solution. Then, the mixture was sonicated for 1h and stirred for 12h under dark environment. The DOX-loaded HMC-SS-COOH nanoparticles were centrifuged and washed gently with pH 7.4 PBS for three times to remove the adsorbed DOX on surface of HMC-SS-COOH. Meanwhile, the DOX concentrations from supernatant and all the washing liquid fractions were collected and measured at 480nm via UV–Vis spectrophotometry. Subsequently, the obtained HMC-SS-COOH/DOX nanoparticles was dispersed in pH 7.4 PBS and activated with EDC and NHS for 1h. Finally, CDPEI were added to the mixture solution and stirred for 36h. The DOX-loaded HMC-SS-CDPEI nanoparticles are defined as HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX. The HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX was gathered by centrifugation, PBS (pH 7.4) washing, and drying. The total quantity of DOX entrapped in the HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX was acquired by subtracting the unloaded DOX in the supernatant during the whole drug loading processes from the amount of DOX added at the beginning. The HMC-SS-CDPEI nanoparticles were prepared as described above without the addition of DOX.

The release profiles of DOX were studied in release media of 0 or 10mM GSH concentration at different pH values, the mixture was placed in a shaking bed at 37°C with a speed of 150rpm. In predefined time intervals, 1mL release solution of different samples were taken out to measure the content of DOX and then pouring the taken solutions back to the dissolution media. The concentration of released DOX was monitored at 480nm by UV–Vis spectrophotometry after the centrifugation at 12000rpm for 3min. For the NIR-triggered release experiment, the samples were illuminated by an 808nm NIR laser (3W) for 3min at designated time intervals prior to measuring the absorbance.

The HMC-SS-CDPEI nanoparticles (5mg) were dispersed in 3mL pH 7.4 PBS in the presence of different concentrations of GSH. After incubated at 37°C for 6h, the supernatants were separated from samples through centrifugation. Then the supernatants were observed under UV light at 360nm and then underwent fluorescence spectrophotometric analysis to obtain the emission spectra, respectively.

6mg HMC-COOH and HMC-SS-CDPEI were dispersed in 3mL distilled water and pH 7.4 PBS, respectively, by sonication to obtain a dispersion with the concentration of 2mgmL−1. At different time intervals, the photographs were taken with a Canon digital camera to assess the stability and dispersity of HMC-COOH and HMC-SS-CDPEI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of cellular photothermal effect induced by NIR
ce:section.ce:para: 4T1 cells were cultured in DMEM at 37°C in a wet atmosphere containing 5% CO2. 4T1 cells were seeded in 6-well plates at a density of 2.0×105/well in order to evaluate cellular photo-thermal capacity and efficiency of HMC-SS-CDPEI. After incubated for 24h, 4T1 cells were separately treated with cell culture media containing samples of HMC-SS-CDPEI at various concentrations of 5, 10, 25 and 50μgmL−1 for 2h. Cells were sequentially incubated at 37°C for an additional 2h, and then the cells were rinsed completely with PBS for three times after the old culture medium was removed. Subsequently, the cells were digested by trypsin and the supernatants were discarded by centrifugation. After that, the cells were re-dispersed in 200µL PBS and irradiated with an 808nm laser at a power density of 2W for 3min. Meanwhile, PBS used as the blank control underwent the same treatment as the HMC-SS-CDPEI nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MTT activity assay for measuring NIR-induced cytotoxicity
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to use the materials for biomedicine applications, the cytotoxicity of free DOX, blank HMC-SS-CDPEI carriers and HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX against 4T1 cells was investigated by MTT assay. 4T1 cells were cultured in 96-well microplates with 100μL of media at a density of 2×104 cells/well for 24h. After incubation, the medium in each well was then replaced with culture medium (150μL) containing DOX to obtain a concentration range of 0.01–10μgmL−1. For NIR-laser irradiation test groups, the suspension of each sample well was taken out after a period of incubation. Then, the cell was washed twice with PBS to remove the uncombined nanoparticles. The cells were treated with fresh culture medium and irradiated with an 808nm NIR laser (2W) for 1min. After NIR light treatment, the cells were further incubated in a humidified atmosphere for a couple of hours. Subsequently, 50µL MTT (2mg/mL) was added to the culture medium and incubation for another 4h. The culture medium was discarded before 150µL DMSO was added to quantify the living cells. Finally, the absorbance of wells was measured by a microplate reader at 570nm. Cells cultured in blank composites medium were taken as the control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of cellular uptake by confocal laser scanning microscopy
ce:section.ce:para: To monitor cellular uptake of HMC-COOH, HMC-SS-CDPEI and HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX, 4T1 cells were seeded onto 24-well plates at a density of 1×105 cells/well and incubated for 24h. Then free DOX and HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX were added to the corresponding cell wells at the DOX concentration of 5μgmL−1. After culturing at 37°C for 2h, the supernatant was discarded and the cells were rinsed three times with fresh PBS and fixed with 4% formaldehyde. Subsequently, the nucleus was stained with DAPI and the cells were imagined by Leica DM-6000CS microscope (Leica Instruments Inc., Wetzlar, Germany).

The specific surface area of mesoporous carries were calculated by a surface area adsorption analyzer (V-Sorb 2800P, Gold APP Instrument Corporation, Beijing, China). The pore characteristic of carries were observed by a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Tecnai G2 F30, FEI, Eindhoven, Netherlands). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) was recorded on a FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker IFS 55, Faellanden, Switzerland). The particle size and zeta potential in aqueous solution were measured by a Nano-ZS90 Nanosizer (Malvern Instruments Ltd., Worcestershire, UK). The UV–vis spectrum was performed on a UV-4802H spectrophotometer (UNIC, Shanghai, China) at room temperature. The Fluorescence spectra was characterized on an F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed by using an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) with an Al-Kα X-ray source.

The PEI and CA mixture solution was transferred to a hydrothermal container and heated at 180°C for 10h. Then, the larger carbon particles were discarded by centrifugation while the fluorescent CDPEI was obtained after dialysis of the supernatant against distilled water to remove the excess reactant and further freeze-drying.

The size and morphology of CDPEI were characterized by TEM and AFM techniques. As shown in Fig. 1 A and B, CDPEI are both mono-dispersed and have a uniform spherical morphology, along with a mean pore size of 3–4nm. As shown in Fig. 1C, no diffraction spots could be seen from FFT, which demonstrated that the CDPEI was noncrystalline. As shown in Fig. 1D, the XPS spectra expressed that the C, O and N element percentages were 71.8%, 18.4% and 9.8%, respectively. The XRD pattern of CDPEI (Fig. S1) displays a wide diffraction peak at 2θ=22.4° ascribed to the amorphous carbon phase [32]. The AFM 3D image and topography image (Fig. 1E and F) further manifest that the morphology of CDPEI is a spherical structure, which is accordance with the TEM results. As shown in Fig. 1G, the average height of CDPEI is 4.2nm which is very close to the diameter of CDPEI observed by TEM, thus it may deduce that CDPEI can act as the gate and prevent the encapsulated drug release early. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S2) was also recorded to identify abundant amide groups on CDPEI, the peaks at 3421, 1651 and 1631cm−1 were attributed to σN-H, σC=O, δN-H, separately.

In Fig. 2 A, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDPEI showed an absorption peak at 240nm and 350nm. The fluorescence spectra of CDPEI revealed a maximum emission at 450nm and a maximum excitation at 350nm. As can be seen in the inserted image, the prepared CDPEI showed the bright blue light under UV light irradiation with excitation wavelength of 360nm. Additionally, for the purpose of investigating the fluorescence characteristics of CDPEI which was exposure to wavelengths of different excitation, the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra of CDPEI were studied as shown in Fig. 2B. The PL emission spectrum of CDPEI exhibited an excitation wavelength-dependent and when the solution of CDPEI was excited at wavelengths from 300nm to 500nm, the emission peak shifted from 430nm to 550nm. This result manifested that the CDPEI was able to emit different fluorescence when excited under diverse excitation wavelengths.

The preparation of HMC-SS-CDPEI nanocomposite with CDPEI as “gatekeepers” linked by cleavable disulfide bonds is presented in Scheme 1. First, the HMC-COOH nanoparticles were allowed to react with Py-SS-NH2 to obtain HMC-SS-Py via the amidation reaction. Subsequently, HMC-SS-COOH was prepared by reacting the HMC-SS-Py with 3-mercaptopropionic acid depending on disulfide bonds exchange reactions. Finally, amino-terminated CDPEI were covalently modified on the surfaces of HMC through the amidation reaction. As shown in the TEM image (Fig. 3 A and B), the average size of prepared HMC-COOH was about 120nm and the average thickness of the shell was about 20nm. And a uniform hollow structure and well-defined mesoporous shell could be observed in the TEM image.

As described in Fig. 4 A and B, nitrogen adsorption–desorption analysis was used to measure the BET isotherms and pore size distribution curves of HMC, HMC-COOH, and HMC-SS-CDPEI. The value of Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area (SBET), pore size distribution (PD) and total pore volume (Vt) are summarized in Table 1 . The SBET and Vt of HMC were 1379.0m2/g and 1.24cm3/g, respectively, and the PD was approximate 2.9nm. The SBET and Vt of HMC-COOH were slightly decreased to 985.3m2/g and 0.91cm3/g after carboxylation. Moreover, the SBET and Vt were significantly decreased after the grafting of CDPEI.

The successful preparation of HMC-SS-CDPEI was testified by different methods. The results of comparing the zeta potentials of HMC-COOH, HMC-SS-COOH and HMC-SS-CDPEI are illustrated in Fig. 4C. The zeta potential of HMC-COOH with abundant carboxyl groups was −30.6mV. After HMC-COOH reacted with Py-SS-NH2 and 3-mercaptopropionic acid, the zeta potentials of HMC-SS-Py and HMC-SS-COOH were changed to −24.7mV and −34.7, respectively. After grafting of amino-terminated CDPEI, the zeta potential of HMC-SS-CDPEI increased markedly to −15.1mV. Further evidence of CDPEI modification on HMC-COOH is confirmed by the FT-IR spectrum. The FT-IR spectra of HMC, HMC-COOH, HMC-SS-COOH and HMC-SS-CDPEI are shown in Fig. 4D. Compared with HMC, the typical stretching vibration of COOH bonds at 1717cm−1 from HMC-COOH indicated successful carboxylation. However, the COOH bonds at 1717cm−1 disappeared after the grafting of CDPEI on the surface of HMC via the amidation reaction. All the results confirmed the successful grafting of CDPEI after each step modification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The drug existing state and loading yield of HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX
ce:section.ce:para: Driven by noncovalent interaction including π–π stacking and electrostatic attraction, the cytotoxic anticancer agent DOX asa model drug was used to be loaded in HMC-SS-COOH in a very efficient way to evaluate the drug loading and release properties of HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX. The original drug carrier HMC-SS-COOH was mixed with DOX in PBS solution by sonication. And then the HMC-SS-COOH/DOX was washed with PBS mildly in order to remove the loosely retained DOX. Subsequently, the CDPEI was attached on the surface of HMC-SS-COOH/DOX through the amide reaction to hinder the premature release of DOX. Based on UV–Vis absorption measurements, the amount of drug molecules stored in HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX was 34%. The reason of high loading capacity of HMC-COOH was ascribed to the huge specific surface area, the hollow cavity, strong adsorption ability and π–π stacking and electrostatic attraction between HMC-COOH and DOX. Moreover, XRD was used to evaluate the physical form of DOX in HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX. As shown in Fig. S3, the raw DOX appeared characteristic and intense crystalline diffraction peaks. Nevertheless, no obvious crystalline peaks of DOX were observed in the HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX, which may provide a hint that the DOX existed in a form of non-crystalline which was attributed to the limitation of the mesoporous structure of HMC [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of in vitro photothermal effect studies induced by NIR
ce:section.ce:para: The time–temperature curves of HMC-SS-CDPEI under NIR irradiation are recorded for photothermal drug delivery. As illustrated in Fig. 5 A, the temperature of HMC-SS-CDPEI suspensions increased markedly at the increasing concentrations from 5 to 100 μgmL−1 revealed that the HMC carrier had the high photothermal conversion efficiency. As shown in Fig. S4, the temperature of 100μgmL−1 HMC-SS-CDPEI suspension raised from 20 to 63°C within 3min when HMC-SS-CDPEI under the NIR irradiation at 2W, while no apparent temperature increase was observed for distilled water at the same condition. According to previous literatures, the tumor cells would ablate heavily when the surrounding temperature ascend to about 42°C [34]. Thus, 100μgmL−1 of HMC-SS-CDPEI was selected to research the heat-generating feature of HMC-SS-CDPEI at different laser power density at 808nm. As can be seen from Fig. 5B, when the laser power density were 0.5W, 1.0W, 2.0W, 3.0W and 4.0W, the final temperature of suspensions elevate to 29°C, 40°C, 61°C, 76°C and 82°C from 20°C, respectively. From these discuss described above, it could be inferred that the HMC-SS-CDPEI was able to convert the NIR photon energy into thermal energy, which kept consistent with other carbon material. That is to say, the HMC-SS-CDPEI nano-composites are suitable for the construction of NIR irradiation-controlled drug delivery platform.

To investigate the redox-sensitive drug release properties of the HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX system, the release behavior of HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX were examined in diverse release media: pH 7.4 PBS, pH 5.0 PBS, pH 7.4 PBS with 10mM GSH and pH 5.0 PBS with 10mM GSH, respectively. The pH 7.4 and 5.0 PBS were used to stimulate normal tissues and tumor microenvironments, separately. As shown in Fig. 6 , merely 9% DOX released from HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX in pH 7.4 PBS within 24h, indicating the good encapsulation efficiency of HMC-SS-CDPEI. And the released DOX increased slightly to 15% pH 5.0 PBS within the same time since the electrostatic interaction between DOX and negative charged carbon was much weaker compared with that in pH 7.4 PBS [35].

On the contrary, when HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX was exposed to 10mM GSH, the cumulative percentage release of DOX was greatly increased to 20% and 35% in pH 7.4 and pH 5.0 PBS within a time interval of 24h. These results indicated that the profile of HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX showed the redox-responsive release property.

To investigate the photothermal influence on drug release, the release profiles of HMC-SS-CDPEI in pH 5.0 and 7.4 PBS were studied in the absence/presence of NIR laser irradiation. As shown in Fig. 7 A and B, a fast drug release was observed once the 808nm laser was utilized during release process and the drug release kinetics exhibited step-like accelerated release property. Finally, the cumulative release percentage of DOX in pH 5.0 with NIR irradiation was 19% as the release process went on for 12h, which was 0.5-folder than that environment without irradiation. Moreover, the NIR irradiation facilitates the drug release from HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX with GSH regardless of the acidic or basic conditions. Thereby, NIR irradiation can produce high cumulative release and fast drug release, which was because the irradiation of 808nm laser caused temperature rise and speeded up molecular movement of DOX and thus greatly accelerated its escape from HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX [36].

As shown in Fig. S5A, DOX asa model drug could emit red fluorescence under 480nm excitation. However, the fluorescence of DOX is quenched when DOX was loaded in HMC-SS-CDPEI, which could attributed to π-π stacking force between DOX and HMC-COOH. Besides, as shown in Fig. S5B, the fluorescence of CDPEI is quenched when grafting CDPEI on the surface of HMC-COOH. To assess the function of CDPEI acting asa drug release “gatekeeper”, as described in Fig. 8 A, the fluorescence spectra of the supernatant CDPEI from HMC-SS-CDPEI after the addition of 0mM, 1mM, 5mM and 10mM of GSH is recorded. However, the PL intensity of the CDPEI is restored with different concentration of GSH (1–10mM) owing to the detachment of CDPEI from the HMC-SS-CDPEI. The developed nanostructured HMC-SS-CDPEI based on fluorescence quenching and recovery of CDPEI was employed for monitoring the drug release. In addition, the supernatants from HMC-SS-CDPEI without GSH have weak fluorescence, while the supernatant of HMC-SS-CDPEI treated with 10mM GSH possessed a blue fluorescence under an irradiation by UV light at 360nm. These results certified that the disulfide-linked CDPEI could be detached from HMC-COOH ascribed to the disruption of the nanocarriers’ integrity and breakage of the disulfide bonds under the high concentration GSH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The advantage of HMC-SS-CDPEI after grafting of CDPEI
ce:section.ce:para: Although HMC-COOH had great potential for stimuli-responsive drug delivery, HMC-COOH suffering from some shortcomings associated with aggregation under physiological conditions have restricted their applications [37]. Thus CDPEI attached on the HMC-COOH could not only act as fluorescence-labeling and cap for trapping the drug molecules within the mesoporous channels but also afford to improve the dispersity and stability of HMC. The hydrodynamic diameter (Dh) of HMC-COOH and HMC-SS-CDPEI was measured in distilled water (Fig. 8B). The diameter of HMC-COOH was 181.4nm (PDI=0.154), which was larger than that observed from the TEM image. Sizes of the carrier from the TEM image were in a dried state without a hydration layer, while the Dh was measured under aqueous conditions. The PDI of HMC-SS-CDPEI was 0.106, which was smaller than that of blank HMC-COOH. The results of the comparison hinted that HMC-SS-CDPEI has a narrow particle size distribution and good dispersity.

The stability of nanoparticles is evaluated through dispersing 6mg HMC-COOH and HMC-SS-CDPEI in 3mL deionized water and pH 7.4 PBS, respectively. As shown in Fig. 8C, HMC-COOH experienced flocculation and sedimentation after dispersion in pH 7.4 PBS, while HMC-COOH was stable in deionized water. On the contrary, the dispersion of HMC-SS-CDPEI was hugely promoted after decorating CDPEI on the surface of HMC-COOH, which could be ascribed to the surface electrostatic repulsions and steric hindrance of hydrophilic CDPEI and nanoparticles.

To investigate the NIR-induced cellular photothermal effect based on HMC-SS-CDPEI, 4T1 cells were incubated with HMC-SS-CDPEI nanoparticles at different concentrations of 5, 10, 25, and 50μg/mL for 2h and then irradiated with 808nm laser for 3min. As shown in Fig. S6, the UV–vis-NIR absorption spectra of HMC-SS-CDPEI indicated that the HMC-SS-CDPEI product exhibited relatively strong NIR absorption at 808nm. As shown in Fig. 9 , no apparent temperature increase was seen for blank cells after NIR irradiation, which manifested that the blank cells could not convert NIR light into heat. Nevertheless, with the concentration of HMC-SS-CDPEI increased, the temperature of cells suspension significantly elevated. This could be explained by that HMC-SS-CDPEI could be uptaked 4T1 cells which endowed the cells with photothermal conversion ability. In addition, with the administrated HMC-SS-CDPEI increased, the quantity of HMC-SS-CDPEI nanoparticles uptaked by 4T1 cells via cell endocytosis also improved. Prominent photo-thermal conversion efficiency and exciting possibilities in treating cancer were evidently substantiated by results of cellular photo-thermal effect.

MTT assay was applied to determine photothermal chemotherapeutic effects of HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX against 4T1 cells under different treatments. As illustrated in Fig. 10 , the blank HMC-SS-CDPEI show no obvious cytotoxicity to 4T1 cells at concentrations ranging from 0.01μgmL−1 to 10μgmL−1 without NIR laser irradiation, which clearly indicated that the HMC-SS-CDPEI expressed negligible cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility. On the contrary, the viability showed a dramatic dose-dependent decrease for cells incubated with HMC-SS-CDPEI exposed to NIR laser, which certified that the HMC-SS-CDPEI had high photothermal conversion efficiency in the NIR regions and could transform the NIR light into heat to “cook” the cancer cells. The free DOX showed concentration-dependent cytotoxicity against 4T1 cells. Besides, no significant viability difference with ap value more than 0.05 was seen through comparing the cell survival rate between the free DOX group and DOX with NIR laser irradiation group, which indicated that the NIR laser produced negligible cytotoxicity to 4T1 tumor cells. Meanwhile, HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX had the highest rate of cell death compared to a single treatment of free DOX or HMC-SS-CDPEI when they are both exposed to NIR laser, which evidently demonstrate that HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX with NIR laser irradiation presented synergetic therapeutic effect on cancer therapy. The mechanism of results could be explained by that the NIR laser irradiation raised the temperature of tumor cells cultured with HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX, and then the elevated temperature could accelerate drug release from HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX and improve the sensitivity of tumor cells to chemotherapy drugs simultaneously [38]. Also, the half-inhibitory concentrations (IC50) of different groups are presented in Table 2 by using SPSS Statistics software. The IC50 value of free DOX group was nearly uniform in presence/absence of NIR irradiation. In contrast, the IC50 value of HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX sharply decreased from 6.717 μgmL−1 without NIR laser to 1.802 μgmL−1 after NIR irradiation. The quantification results also exhibited that the IC50 value of the HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX with NIR irradiation was much lower than that of free DOX and HMC-SS-CDPEI with NIR laser irradiation. These results further certified that the combination of chemotherapy and photothermal therapy (PTT) based on HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX nanocomposites was a more efficient therapeutic regimen against cancer cells than either chemotherapy or PTT alone.

For majority diagnosis and treatment systems, the bioimaging ability of fluorescent nanocarriers is a very significant prerequisite that impact cell internalization and localization of nanomaterials. Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) was carried out to evaluate the cellular uptake efficiency and specificity of DOX/HMC-SS-CDPEI for intracellular therapeutic applications. As shown in Fig. 11 , the fluorescence microscopy images are collected for blue (DAPI) and red (DOX) after incubating 4T1 cells with DOX and HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX for 2h. The red fluorescence of DOX is observed in the region of the nucleus under an excitation wavelength of 561nm, which is consistent with the blue region labeled by DAPI when excited with 408nm laser pulses. The results indicated that DOX molecules can penetrate into the cells and ultimately entered into the nucleus to kill the cells. Besides, the overlapped fluorescence in the merged image was also found mainly in the cell cytoplasm, implying that the HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX could be taken up by 4T1 cells.

On the basis of the previously related reports [18,35,39], this present work has developed a multifunctional drug delivery platform based on HMC to realize the NIR light and redox dual-responsive controlled drug release and chemo-photothermal synergistic therapy. The fluorescent CDPEI used as the capping was grafted on the surface of HMC via cleavable disulfide bonds to prevent the leakage of the drugs and improve dispersity under physiological conditions, which was similar to those reported carbonaceous dots [26,40]. However, the fluorescence of the CDPEI was quenched when being grafted to the HMC, while it was restored when the CDPEI detached away from HMC. Thus, the system could become a potential candidate to monitor the drug release process and determine the location of nanoparticles inside the cells. The in vitro release results indicated that HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX exhibited smart drug release in response to redox and NIR light stimuli. Cell viability assay showed that HMC-SS-CDPEI/DOX possessed a synergistic effect by combination of chemotherapy and photothermal therapy under the NIR irradiation. The present work suggested an exploration of NIR and redox dual-responsive drug delivery system of HMC-SS-CDPEI asa promising visible platform for chemo-photothermal synergistic therapy. However, the CDPEI could not be effectively used for in vivo image due to the weak penetration of the blue-green emission light. Thus, further study on the CDs with the emission wavelength in the red and near-infrared regions is under way to overcome this drawback.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81603058 and 81473165), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2017M611270), Career Development Program for Young Teachers in Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (No. ZQN2016025) and Doctoral Research Funding of Liaoning Province (No. 201601146).

ce:section.ce:para: We have no conflicts of interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2017.08.010.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mater. Sci. Eng. C Mater. Biolog. Appl.
